question: What does fault code F2 mean on a PowerFlex 40 VFD?
ideal_answer: F2 on the PowerFlex 40 indicates an overcurrent fault. The drive detected current exceeding the overcurrent trip level. Common causes include: shorted motor leads, ground fault in motor cabling, motor stall condition, or undersized drive for the load. Action: check motor wiring for shorts or grounds, verify motor FLA matches drive rating, inspect for mechanical binding causing stall.
contexts: The PowerFlex 40 fault code F2 indicates Overcurrent — the drive output current exceeded the overcurrent trip level.|Common causes of F2: shorted motor leads, ground fault between drive and motor, motor stall, or drive undersized for load.|Action for F2: disconnect motor leads at drive and megohmeter test. Check for mechanical binding. Verify motor FLA does not exceed drive rated current.

question: How do I reset a Siemens 3RT2 contactor after an overload trip?
ideal_answer: To reset a Siemens 3RT2 contactor after overload: 1) Identify and resolve the root cause of the overload (check motor current draw, mechanical binding, ambient temperature). 2) Wait for the thermal overload relay to cool (typically 2-5 minutes). 3) Press the blue RESET button on the 3RU2 or 3RB3 overload relay mounted below the contactor. 4) If using manual reset mode, the relay must be physically pressed. If auto-reset is configured, it resets after cooldown.
contexts: Siemens 3RT2 contactors pair with 3RU2 thermal overload relays. After trip: identify root cause, allow relay to cool, press blue RESET button on relay.|3RT2 overload relays support manual or auto reset. Manual reset requires pressing the blue button after thermal element cools. Auto-reset re-engages after cooldown period.|Before resetting: verify motor current draw is within FLA rating. Check for mechanical binding, phase imbalance, or high ambient temperature.

question: CompactLogix 1769-L33ER is showing a red FAULT LED — what should I check?
ideal_answer: A solid red FAULT LED on the CompactLogix 1769-L33ER indicates a major non-recoverable fault. Check: 1) Open RSLogix 5000/Studio 5000 and read the fault code from Controller Properties > Major Faults tab. 2) Common causes: power-up fault requiring firmware update, memory corruption, hardware failure, or unrecoverable program fault. 3) Try cycling power. If fault persists, note the fault code (Type/Code) and contact Rockwell support. 4) Check if the battery (1756-BA2) is dead — a dead battery causes memory loss on power cycle.
contexts: CompactLogix 1769-L33ER solid red FAULT LED indicates a major non-recoverable fault. Check Controller Properties > Major Faults in Studio 5000 for the specific fault type and code.|Common 1769-L33ER faults: Type 1 (power-up), Type 3 (I/O), Type 4 (communication), Type 6 (program). Power cycle clears Type 1. Type 6 requires program fix.|The 1769-L33ER uses a 1756-BA2 battery for memory backup. Dead battery + power loss = memory corruption = solid red FAULT on next power-up.

question: What parameters do I need to set for basic motor control on a GS20 VFD?
ideal_answer: For basic motor control on the GS20 VFD, set these parameters: P01 (Max Output Frequency) = motor nameplate Hz (typically 60Hz). P02 (Source of Frequency Command) = 0 for keypad, 1 for external analog. P03 (Source of Run Command) = 0 for keypad, 1 for external terminals. P04 (Motor Nameplate Amps) = motor FLA from nameplate. P05 (Motor Nameplate Voltage) = motor rated voltage. P06 (Motor Nameplate RPM) = motor rated speed. P07 (Accel Time) and P08 (Decel Time) = seconds for ramp up/down.
contexts: GS20 VFD basic parameters: P01=Max Frequency (60Hz), P02=Frequency Source (0=keypad, 1=analog), P03=Run Source (0=keypad, 1=terminal), P04=Motor FLA, P05=Motor Voltage, P06=Motor RPM.|GS20 P07=Acceleration Time (seconds), P08=Deceleration Time (seconds). Default 10s each. Reduce for fast response, increase for high-inertia loads.|Before starting: set P04 (Motor FLA) to match nameplate EXACTLY. Incorrect FLA setting disables electronic thermal overload protection.

question: Allen-Bradley 193-1EF overload relay keeps tripping — what should I check?
ideal_answer: If the 193-1EF overload relay keeps tripping: 1) Measure actual motor current with a clamp meter on all three phases — compare to relay setting and motor FLA. 2) Check for phase imbalance (>5% between phases indicates supply issue). 3) Verify the relay current dial matches motor FLA (not locked rotor amps). 4) Check ambient temperature — high ambient reduces relay trip threshold. 5) Inspect motor for mechanical binding, bearing failure, or shaft misalignment causing excess current draw. 6) If relay is Class 10, consider upgrading to Class 20 for high-inertia loads with long acceleration times.
contexts: Allen-Bradley 193-1EF is a bimetallic overload relay for 0.1-32A range. Trips on sustained overcurrent. Dial must be set to motor FLA from nameplate.|Repeated 193-1EF trips: check all three phase currents with clamp meter. Phase imbalance >5% indicates power supply issue, not motor fault.|193-1EF is Class 10 (trips in 10 seconds at 6x FLA). High-inertia loads with long acceleration may need Class 20 relay (193-1EG series) to avoid nuisance trips.

question: The Ignition tag HR100 shows 0 RPM and HR101 shows 0.0 A but run coil C0 is active — what does this indicate on the GS10 VFD?
ideal_answer: Zero speed (HR100=0 RPM) and zero current (HR101=0 A) with the run coil active (C0=1) on a GS10 VFD indicates the drive received a run command but the motor is not responding. Most likely causes in order: 1) GS10 drive fault — check HR fault register or display panel for active fault code. 2) Drive is in Ready state but an enable input is missing — verify terminal S1 (Enable) is wired and active. 3) Frequency reference (HR105 or analog input) is zero — check P02 frequency source and verify the setpoint is non-zero. 4) Motor output wiring open or disconnected — zero current with run command rules out motor stall; the drive is not energizing the output. Check T1/T2/T3 terminals. Action: read the active fault code from the GS10 keypad or HR fault register. Clear the fault, verify enable wiring, then re-issue a run command.
contexts: GS10 VFD Modbus register HR100 = motor speed in RPM (read from output frequency). Zero RPM with active run coil means drive did not accelerate despite command.|HR101 = output current in Amps. Zero current with run command and zero RPM confirms drive output stage is not energizing the motor — distinguishes from a motor stall (which shows high current at zero speed).|GS10 requires both a run command (digital terminal or Modbus coil C0) AND a valid frequency reference (P02 source non-zero) to run. Missing enable or zero reference produces this symptom.|Common GS10 faults that latch without motor movement: CE10 (comm timeout before first run), OC (overcurrent at start — clears after fault reset), and EF (external fault if terminal S5 wired as external fault input).

question: The DURApulse GS10 VFD on conveyor CV-101 tripped an oC overcurrent fault again. What does the work-order history tell us and what should I check first?
ideal_answer: CV-101 has a recurring oC overcurrent history in its work orders, so treat this as a repeat mechanical-load problem before condemning the drive. Prior evidence: [WO 1042] (2026-05-03) traced the same oC fault to a worn drive belt causing motor bind — the belt was replaced and running current returned below FLA; [WO 0987] (2026-03-18) traced an earlier oC to debris increasing roller drag. Both point to rising mechanical load, not a drive hardware fault. Check first, in order: 1) belt tension and wear (the [WO 1042] root cause), 2) roller/bearing drag and debris (the [WO 0987] root cause), 3) confirm the motor FLA parameter still matches the nameplate. Only after ruling out mechanical load should you reset and monitor the drive. [Source: DURApulse GS10 — Overcurrent (oC)]
contexts: [WO 1042] 2026-05-03 (closed): CV-101 GS10 oC overcurrent fault -- Replaced worn drive belt causing motor bind; reset drive, verified running current below FLA.|[WO 0987] 2026-03-18 (closed): CV-101 GS10 recurring oC fault -- Removed debris from conveyor rollers that was increasing mechanical load.|GS10 fault oC = overcurrent: drive output current exceeded the trip level. Mechanical binding, belt or roller drag, or a load increase are common causes of a repeat oC on a conveyor.|Prior work orders are citable as [WO <number>] when they explain the current symptom.
